coredata.dc:title: Oxygen/nitrogen-related surface states controlled carbon nanodots with tunable full-color luminescence: Mechanism and bio-imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Lei
Zhang, Xuehui
Yang, Kun
Wang, Luning
Lee, Chun-Sing
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent carbon nanodots (CNDs) have exhibited attractive potential for biomedical applications due to their intriguing luminescent properties and good biocompatibility. One interesting property of CNDs is that they typically show different emission colors upon optical excitation using different wavelengths. However, it is still a challenge to obtain emissions covering the entire visible spectrum with comparable intensity with only one type of CND. Herein, CNDs with tunable full-color luminescence are successful prepared by a solvothermal approach, show broad absorption (200–700 nm) and fluorescence emission (nearly cover the entire visible region: from 400 to 700 nm) with comparable fluorescence intensities. The oxidation (o-CNDs) and reduction (r-CNDs) of CNDs surface would lead to the shifting of photoluminescence band into blue/green and red-light region, respectively. Further spectroscopic analyses and structural characterizations of CNDs (pristine state), o-CNDs (oxidation state) and r-CNDs (reduction state) demonstrate that O-related defect states (CO) and N-related defect states (CN) on surface of CNDs should be responsible for blue/green and red fluorescence emissions, respectively. Moreover, the CNDs are found to be biocompatible, as verified by in vitro cells assay and in vivo histological analysis. In vivo fluorescence imaging assay demonstrates that CNDs can be excreted through intestine and bladder system. The tunable full-color emission, good biocompatibility and metabolism suggest that the CNDs are promising fluorescent probes for biomedical applications.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CNDs with tunable full-color luminescence, o-CNDs and r-CNDs
In vivo bioimaging and toxicity assessment
Characterizations of CNDs with tunable full-color luminescence

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optical and morphology of CNDs with tunable full-color luminescence. (a) UV–vis absorbance spectrum of CNDs diluted suspension; (b) Photoluminescence spectra of the CNDs under different excitation wavelengths; (c) Images of water drop containing CNDs under excitation wavelength of 405, 488, 514 and 543 nm; (d) TEM images of CNDs; (e) Size distribution histogram (5.2 nm); (f) High-resolution TEM images of one CND with lattice spacing of 0.21 nm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The relationship between surface chemical state and fluorescence emission in CNDs, o-CNDs and r-CNDs. Two-dimensional fluorescence topographical maps of the (a) CNDs, (b) o-CNDs, (c) r-CNDs; High-resolution XPS of C1s spectra of the (d) CNDs, (e) o-CNDs, (f) r-CNDs; (g) UV–vis absorption spectra of the CNDs, o-CNDs, and r-CNDs in aqueous suspension; (h) FT-IR spectra of the CNDs, o-CNDs, and r-CNDs samples; (i) Raman spectra of the CNDs, o-CNDs, and r-CNDs samples under the excitation of a 633 nm laser. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Fluorescence emission mechanism of CNDs with tunable full-color luminescence. (a) Schematic presentation and the related structural model of the CNDs with tunable full-color luminescence. (b) Schematic illustration for proposed energy level and electron transition diagrams of o-CNDs and r-CNDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

CNDs with tunable full-color luminescence for live cell multicolor bioimaging. (a) Cell viability of A549 and HeLa cells after incubation with different CNDs concentrations for 12 and 24 h. (b) Fluorescence images of A549 and HeLa cells treated with CNDs under excitation wavelength of 405, 488, 514 and 543 nm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

CNDs with tunable full-color luminescence for in vivo bio-imaging. (a) Fluorescent pseudo-colored image of CNDs aqueous suspension with different CNDs concentrations; (b) Quantitative fluorescence intensity versus CNDs concentration; (c) Real time in vivo imaging of nude mice with intravenous injection of CNDs at different time points (I: Thoracic region, II: area of liver, III: area of small intestine, IV: area of large intestine, V: bladder region.); (d) H&E stained tissue slices (Heart, liver, lung, kidney and spleen) of mice injected with CNDs aqueous suspension (dose of 25 mg/kg) and untreated mice at 1 and 7 days. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
XPS data analyses of the C1s spectra of CNDs, o-CNDs and r-CNDs.
XPS elemental analysis results of CNDs, o-CNDs and r-CNDs.

head.ce:title: Oxygen/nitrogen-related surface states controlled carbon nanodots with tunable full-color luminescence: Mechanism and bio-imaging

Hong Kong SAR & City University of Hong Kong Shenzhen Research Institute

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center of Super-Diamond and Advanced Films (COSDAF) and Department of Chemistry, City University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong SAR & City University of Hong Kong Shenzhen Research Institute, Shenzhen, Guangdong, 518057, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Dental Materials & Dental Medical Devices Testing Center, National Engineering Laboratory for Digital and Material Technology of Stomatology, Peking University School and Hospital of Stomatology, Beijing, 100081, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Dental Materials & Dental Medical Devices Testing Center

Peking University School and Hospital of Stomatology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Dental Materials & Dental Medical Devices Testing Center, National Engineering Laboratory for Digital and Material Technology of Stomatology, Peking University School and Hospital of Stomatology, Beijing, 100081, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent carbon nanodots (CNDs) have exhibited attractive potential for biomedical applications due to their intriguing luminescent properties and good biocompatibility. One interesting property of CNDs is that they typically show different emission colors upon optical excitation using different wavelengths. However, it is still a challenge to obtain emissions covering the entire visible spectrum with comparable intensity with only one type of CND. Herein, CNDs with tunable full-color luminescence are successful prepared by a solvothermal approach, show broad absorption (200–700 nm) and fluorescence emission (nearly cover the entire visible region: from 400 to 700 nm) with comparable fluorescence intensities. The oxidation (o-CNDs) and reduction (r-CNDs) of CNDs surface would lead to the shifting of photoluminescence band into blue/green and red-light region, respectively. Further spectroscopic analyses and structural characterizations of CNDs (pristine state), o-CNDs (oxidation state) and r-CNDs (reduction state) demonstrate that O-related defect states (CO) and N-related defect states (CN) on surface of CNDs should be responsible for blue/green and red fluorescence emissions, respectively. Moreover, the CNDs are found to be biocompatible, as verified by in vitro cells assay and in vivo histological analysis. In vivo fluorescence imaging assay demonstrates that CNDs can be excreted through intestine and bladder system. The tunable full-color emission, good biocompatibility and metabolism suggest that the CNDs are promising fluorescent probes for biomedical applications.

Carbon nanodots with tunable full-color luminescence can be controlled by oxygen/nitrogen-related surface state, and can serve as optical fluorescence nanoprobes in multicolor bio-imaging.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanodot (CND) is considered as a new class of fluorescent nanomaterial owing to their intriguing luminescent properties, good biocompatibility, excellent chemical and photo stability [1–5]. Due to these merits, CNDs have been shown to have many potential applications, such as bio-imaging, sensing, catalysis, and optoelectronic devices [6–8]. Among these various interesting properties of CNDs, their excitation-dependent fluorescence from blue to red have drawn much attention for their potential to realize a wide color gamut or even full color imaging. Multiple color emissions from CNDs are currently achieved by tuning the synthesis parameters to obtained different CNDs or via using excitations of different wavelength on the same CND. For example, Fan’s group have prepared bright multicolor bandgap CNDs by controlling carbonization of diaminonaphthalene and citric acid [7]. Xiong’s group obtained multicolor CNDs with narrowed emission peak widths by modulating π-electron system and graphitic nitrogen content [9]. Pang’s group synthesized photoluminescence-tunable CNDs with fluorescence emission ranging from blue to red by surface oxidation of carbon fibers [10]. On the other hand, several groups have also shown that the same CND can also give different emission colors upon excitations with different wavelengths [11–14]. However, the multiple color emissions obtained via different excitation wavelengths are accompanied with considerable intensity changes. For practical application, it will be convenient if only one kind of CNDs is employed and the tunable full-color luminescence can be achieved with comparable intensities just by changing excitation wavelength.

To achieve the above aims, it is important to investigate the factors that influence fluorescence of CNDs. Like the case of semiconductor quantum dots, several factors including particle size effect [15,16], surface chemical states [17,18] and heteroatom doping [19,20] have strong influences on the fluorescence of CNDs. Among these factors, especially the surface chemical states (including surface linkage groups) play a vital role in regulating fluorescence intensity and band shape. For instance, Sun’s group obtained a series of CNDs with multiple color emission (from 430 to 630 nm) by modulating the extent of graphitization and the amount of surface carboxyl groups [4]. Yang’s group demonstrated that oxygenous functional groups on surface of the prepared CNDs are responsible for green fluorescence emission, while the blue fluorescence originates from the intrinsic state of the carbon cores sp2 structure [17]. Xiong’s group reported that fluorescence of CNDs shift to longer wavelength as the extent of their surface oxidation increase [21]. These conducive reports suggest that both carbon cores and surface states determine the fluorescence behavior of CNDs, therefore optimizing the surface chemical states (including redox states and functional groups on surface) will provide inspiration for obtaining CNDs with tunable full-color luminescence.

In this work, CNDs with tunable full-color luminescence over the entire visible spectrum is successful achieved via a simple solvothermal approach. We further studied how surface chemical state of the CNDs can affect their fluorescent properties. It is demonstrated that O-related defect states (CO) and N-related defect states (CN) on the surface of CNDs should be responsible for blue/green and red fluorescence emissions, respectively. Moreover, the CNDs are found to be biocompatible, as verified by in vitro cells assay and in vivo histological analysis and show great potentials as a multicolor bio-imaging reagent. In vivo fluorescence imaging assay demonstrates that CNDs can be excreted through intestine and bladder system. The tunable full-color emission, good biocompatibility and metabolism suggest that the CNDs are promising fluorescent probes for biomedical applications.

Formamide, citric acid, urea and sodium borohydride were supplied by Sigma-Aldrich. Dialysis tubing with molecular weight cut-off range of 1000 Da was supplied by Spectrum (USA). All reagents were used without further purification. Deionized water (Millipore Milli-Q grade) with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ was used in all experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CNDs with tunable full-color luminescence, o-CNDs and r-CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: The CNDs with tunable full-color luminescence were prepared via a solvothermal approach. Briefly, citric acid (1 g) and urea (2 g) was dissolved in formamide (20 mL), and stirred for 10 min. The transparent solution was then transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave. After heating at 160 °C for 12 h and cooling the autoclave to room temperature naturally, a black solution was obtained. The reaction mixture was then diluted with deionized water (5-fold) and dialyzed against deionized water using a dialysis tubing for 24 h to remove the unreacted starting materials and small molecular weight reaction products. The dialyzed aqueous dispersion was centrifuged (16000 r/min for 20 min) and filtered through a 0.22 μm Millipore membrane to remove large particles. Dark red powder (∼120 mg) of (hereafter referred as CNDs) was obtained by lyophilization.

Oxidized CNDs (o-CNDs) were prepared by adding concentrated HNO3 (1 mL) into a CND aqueous dispersion (5 mL, 10 mg/mL) and then stirred for 5 min. The reaction mixture was dialyzed against deionized water for 12 h to remove the unreacted HNO3. The dialyzed dispersion was centrifuged (16000 r/min for 20 min) and filtered through a 0.22 μm Millipore membrane to remove large particles. Finally, o-CNDs in a form of dark yellow powder was obtained by lyophilization.

Reductive CNDs (r-CNDs) were prepared through adding NaBH4 (100 mg) into a CND aqueous dispersion (5 mL, 10 mg/mL) and stirred for 5 h. The reaction mixture was then dialyzed against deionized water for 12 h to remove the unreacted materials. The dialyzed dispersion was centrifuged (16000 r/min for 20 min) and filtered through a 0.22 μm Millipore membrane to remove large particles. Finally, r-CND was obtained as red powder upon lyophilization.

UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured with a spectrophotometer (UV-1700, Shimadzu, Japan) and collected from 200 to 800 nm. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured on a spectrofluorometer (FLUORMAX-4, USA) at ambient conditions. The time-resolved fluorescence decay curves were measured at room temperature with a FLS980 time resolved single-photon counting (TCSPC) system. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were pictured from a high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEM-2100F, JEOL). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained on a Nicolet iS50 ranging from 4000 to 400 cm−1 (KBr pellets). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were performed on an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ULVAC-PHI, Quantro SXM). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was performed on Bruker Dimension Icon. Raman spectra of the as-prepared samples were recorded at ambient temperature on a Renishaw inVia micro-Raman system under 633 nm excitation. Fluorescent quantum yields were measured on a QE-2100 quantum efficiency measurement system (Japan Otsuka Electronics).

Cytotoxicity was evaluated using the MTT assay against A549 and HeLa cancer cells. These cancer cells (1 × 105 cells/mL) were seeded onto 96-well plates and cultured with a DMEM medium in a 5% CO2 humidified atmosphere at 37 °C for 24 h. These cells were then incubated with CNDs of different concentrations (0, 50, 100, 200 or 400 μg/mL) for another 12 or 24 h, respectively. The medium containing CNDs was removed and washed with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution three times. 20 μL of MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was added to each well and incubated for another 4 h to allow formation of formazan, followed by removing the medium and 200 μL of DMSO was then added. Optical density (OD) value at 490 nm was measured. Cell viability was expressed as percentage of OD values relative to a control sample which contained no CNDs. Experiments were performed in triplicates, with six replicate wells for each sample per assay.

A549 and HeLa cancer cells (2 × 104 cells/mL) were respectively seeded onto 96-well plates and cultured with a DMEM medium in a 5% CO2 humidified atmosphere at 37 °C for 24 h. CNDs was re-dispersed into the medium solution with a concentration of 100 μg/mL and filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane. The fluorescent medium solution was then added into the 96-well plates. After 12 h of incubation, the medium was removed and the cells were washed three times and kept in PBS solution for multicolor cell bioimaging. Fluorescence images were taken with a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Leica TCS-SP5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo bioimaging and toxicity assessment
ce:section.ce:para: All experimental protocols involving animals were approved by the animal care and use regulations (Institutional Review Board of Peking University School and Hospital of Stomatology) and the experiments were conducted under control of Peking University’s Guidelines for Animal Experimentation. Nude mice (BALB/c) were 6 weeks of age, weighed 18–23 g and acclimatized for 5 days after arrival. In brief, BALB/c nude mice were intravenously injected with 200 μL of the CNDs aqueous solution at a dosage of 25 mg/kg or with saline as the control. The in vivo fluorescent images were taken at various time points (0, 5, 15, 30, 60, 120 min post-injection) using a Maestro In-Vivo Imaging System (CRi, USA) under an excitation light of 570 nm, an emission filter of 680 nm and exposure time of 0.2 s. One day and seven days after intravenous injection, major tissues (heart, liver, lung, kidney and spleen) were harvested and fixed in 4% paraformaldehyde buffer solutions overnight. The tissue samples were embedded in paraffin blocks and sectioned into 5 μm slices, followed by hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining. The stained sections were observed using a microscope (Nikon Eclipse 90i).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of CNDs with tunable full-color luminescence
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 a shows an absorption spectrum of a water dispersion of the as-prepared CNDs. The most important difference from previously reported CNDs is that the present sample shows a much wide absorption covering from UV to almost the entire visible range [11,13,14]. The absorption peak at ∼220 nm is attributed to typical π→π* electronic transitions in aromatic π systems [22]. On the other hand, the weaker absorption over 300–650 nm, corresponding to n→π* electronic transitions of CO and CN bonds in the present CNDs. Similar to previously reported CNDs [12,13], the present CNDs also show tunable fluorescence emission peaks according to the excitation wavelength (Fig. 1b). It is interesting that the fluorescence emission of the present CNDs not only covers the entire visible spectrum, but also their fluorescence intensities are comparable under excitation with different wavelengths. While the excitation-dependent fluorescence emission can also be obtained in previously reported CNDs, the shifts of fluorescence emission center often involve the distinct attenuation in fluorescence intensity. Performance of recently reported state-of-the-art full-color luminescence CNDs are compared and summarized in Table S1. It can be seen that newly full-color luminescence in this work is achieved upon simply tuning excitation wavelengths on the same CNDs. However, in other reported literatures, full-color (or multicolor) emission from CNDs are achieved by tuning the synthesis parameters to obtained different CNDs with various emissions [21,23–28]. For practical application, it will be convenient if only one kind of CNDs is employed and tunable full-color luminescence can be achieved with comparable intensities just by changing excitation wavelength. To further give a clear enough presentation on these advantages (comparable intensities over broad emission) of full-color CNDs, another blue emission CNDs have been prepared using a typical hydrothermal process. Fig. S1a shows that the blue emission CNDs mainly absorb UV light and has negligible absorption in the visible range. Fig. S1b shows that as the excitation wavelength changing from 340 to 500 nm, the emission peak shifts from 480 to 540 nm and the emission intensity changes by over 30 times. On the other hand, the full-color luminescence CND shows board absorption from UV to over 700 nm (Fig. 1a). As the excitation wavelength changing from 350 to 590 nm, the emission peak shifts from 420 to 650 nm and the emission intensity changes by only 2.3 times. Fig. 1c show fluorescent images of a CND water dispersion under different wavelength excitations. Fig. 1d shows a typical TEM image of the as-prepared CNDs, showing the CNDs sample contains nanoparticles which are uniformly-sized spheres with an average size of 5.2 nm (Fig. 1e). Fig. 1f is a high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of one CND showing a distinct crystalline structure with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm which matches well to the spacing of the (100) plane of graphitic carbon [29,30]. A representative AFM image (Fig. S2) further confirms that the CND sample mostly consists of particles with height in the range of 3–6 nm.

Luminescence of the CND sample is further custom-tuned with oxidation or reduction treatments with respectively concentrated HNO3 and NaBH4 to obtain the o-CNDs (oxidation state) and the r-CNDs (reduction state) samples. Interestingly, compared with the as-prepared CND sample which exhibits broad emission covering nearly the entire visible region, the o-CNDs and the r-CNDs show obvious blue-shifted emission and red-shifted emission, respectively (Fig. 2 a–c). To investigate surface chemical state and functional groups of the CND, the o-CND and the r-CND samples, XPS measurements was carried out. Three dominant peaks at 285, 399 and 532 eV can be observed in their XPS survey spectra and be ascribed to the C1s, N1s and O1s (Fig. S3), indicating that the three samples consisted of the same carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen elements. High-resolution C1s spectra of the three samples are shown in Fig. 2d–f. These C1s peaks are deconvoluted into four peaks, which represent graphitic sp2 carbon (C–C/CC, 284.6 eV), sp3 carbon (C–N/C–O, 285.7 eV), carbonyl carbon (CN/CO, 287.7 eV) and carboxyl carbon (COOH, 288.8 eV) [31,32]. While, the CNDs show all four peaks (Fig. 2d), the o-CND sample does not show the CN/CO peak while the r-CND sample does not show the COOH peak. Detailed changes in proportion of these function groups are summarized in Table 1 . In high-resolution C1s spectrum of o-CND sample (Fig. 2e), the proportion of carboxyl carbons (COOH, 288.8 eV) significantly increase (from 15.1% to 24.4%, Table 1) while carbonyl carbon (CN/CO, 287.7 eV) almost disappear, thus implying the oxidation of CO groups for forming COOH groups. On the contrary, the proportion of carboxyl carbons (COOH, 288.8 eV) in r-CND sample disappear while carbonyl carbon (CN/CO, 287.7 eV) obviously increase (from 7.7% to 28.7%, Table 1) due to reduction of COOH groups (Fig. 2f). The optical properties and PL mechanism of CNDs have been reported to depend greatly on their surface-state [21], conjugated π-domains [33] and size effect (size dependent shape and corresponding edge variations) [34]. Therefore, particle sizes of CNDs, o-CNDs and r-CNDs are investigated using AFM measurement. AFM topography images as shown in Fig. S2 and Table S2 demonstrate that CNDs, o-CNDs and r-CNDs are uniformly-sized spheres with an average size of 5.4 ± 1.5, 5.2 ± 1.2 and 5.7 ± 1.1 nm, respectively. These results indicate that the particle sizes of o-CNDs and r-CNDs have not significantly changed during the oxidization/reduction processes; and the changes in optical behavior (including absorption and fluorescence emission) is not caused by size difference but mainly due to change in surface state. The present results confirm that the oxidation and the reduction treatments can both significantly tune the populations of surface functional groups.

For the o-CNDs, concentrated HNO3 is used for strong oxidant. The CN bonds on the surface of pristine CNDs are transformed into C–N bonds [35,36]; the amide and hydroxyl groups are oxidized into carboxyl groups under strong oxidation condition (Fig. S4). It will result in the proportion of carboxyl carbon significantly increase and CN carbon almost disappear, which is consistent with the XPS results (Table 1). For the r-CNDs, NaBH4 is used for strong reductant. During this reduction process, The C–N bonds on the surface of pristine CNDs are transformed into CN bonds [35,36]; and the carboxyl and carbonyl groups are reduced into hydroxyl groups (Fig. S4). It will result in the proportion of CN carbon significantly increase and carboxyl carbon almost disappear, which is also consistent with the XPS results (Table 1).

The N1s band of CND, o-CND and r-CND samples can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 399.5 and 401.3 eV, representing amino N and pyrrolic N, respectively (Fig. S5). The O1s band also have two peaks at 531.4 and at 533.3 eV for CO and C–O, respectively (Fig. S6).

Furthermore, the O content and O/C ratio obviously increase (from 27.9% to 35.2%, from 45.4% to 60.1%) when CNDs was subjected to strong oxidation; while N content and N/C ratio obviously increase (from 6.1% to 14.9%, from 17.6% to 23.6%) when CNDs was subjected to reduction, as shown in Table 2 . These results further confirm that the relative change between N and O element (induced by oxidation and reduction) on surface of CNDs is responsible for the PL shifting. The nitrogen content on surface of o-CNDs significantly reduce (from 10.8% to 6.1%) as the C–N and CN bonds are oxidized into carboxyl groups. On the contrary, the nitrogen content on surface of r-CNDs significantly increase (from 10.8% to 14.9%) due to the significantly reduced oxygen content during the reduction process. Absorption spectra for the CND, the o-CND and the r-CND samples are shown in Fig. 2g. For the o-CNDs, the absorption bands (300–400 nm) related to the CO groups increase while the band assigned to the CN groups disappears owing to oxidation of surface groups [37–39]. In contrast, the absorption (480–600 nm) associated with CN groups in the r-CNDs increases owing to reduction of surface groups by NaBH4 [37–39]. In addition, FTIR spectra were measured to identify the functional groups on surface of CNDs, o-CNDs and r-CNDs (Fig. 2h). Compared with the spectrum of CNDs, stretching vibrations of O–H at 3220 cm−1 are observed in the o-CND sample; while stretching vibrations of N–H at 3400 cm−1 obviously increase in the r-CND sample. Besides, a broad band from 1500 to 1800 cm−1 encompassing two stretching vibrations is also observed in the spectra: stretching vibrations of CO at 1700 cm−1 in carboxyl group and CN at 1620 cm−1 [15,40]. Raman spectra were utilized to characterize the intrinsic structure of the three samples (Fig. 2i). The G band at 1585 cm−1 is related to the vibration of sp2-bonded carbon atoms in a 2D hexagonal lattice of a graphite cluster. The D band at 1365 cm−1 was assigned to the disordered carbon structure [41]. Notably, in the Raman spectra of r-CNDs, a new signal peak appears at around 1635 cm−1, which is assigned to CN group [42].

The photoluminescence decays for the emissions at 500 and 620 nm were further measured to identify various emission origins (Fig. S7). The averaged lifetimes of pristine CNDs are found to be 8.83 and 4.89 ns under excitation with 375 and 485 nm lasers (Table S3), which indicate that the blue/green and red fluorescence emissions are associated with different emitting states [37,38]. Moreover, the averaged lifetimes of o-CNDs (emissions at 500 nm) and r-CNDs (emissions at 620 nm) is calculated to be 8.40 and 3.91 ns, which is similar to that of pristine CNDs at 500 and 620 nm. The above results indicate that the blue/green and red fluorescence emissions are associated with different states and can be tuned by oxidization/reduction (controlling surface states). The quantum yields of the CND sample are determined to be 13.1, 20.0, and 28.1% under 350, 440, and 530 nm excitation, respectively (Table S4), demonstrating the potential for bio-imaging over a broad excitation wavelength region [43].

Based on the above analysis and the possible chemical reactions occurred on the surface of CNDs, a possible structure of the CNDs and related energy-state model responsible for the multi-state absorption and tunable full-color luminescence are proposed, as illustrated in Fig. 3 . During the solvothermal reaction, citric acid (serving as a carbon source) and urea (serving as a nitrogen source) can constitute the CNDs by dehydration, carbonization/nitridation and aromatization reactions (Fig. 3a). On the surface of the CNDs, plentiful functional groups (such as –CONH2, COOH, C–OH, CO and CN) as the O/N-related surface states provide broad visible absorptions and tunable full-color luminescence. As shown in Fig. 3b, the absorption bands in region of 200–300 nm are assigned to the π→π* (HOMO→LUMO) transition of sp2 carbon domain in the core matrix. For the o-CNDs, the CN bonds on the surface of pristine CNDs are transformed into C–N bonds; the amide and hydroxyl groups are oxidized into carboxyl groups under strong oxidation condition. For the r-CNDs, the C–N bonds on the surface of pristine CNDs are transformed into CN bonds; the carboxyl and carbonyl groups are reduced into hydroxyl groups during this reduction process. Therefore, oxygen and nitrogen doping will induce O-related or N-related defect states located in the gap between the HOMO and LUMO, and N-related defect states involve lower energy state [11,39,44]. The absorption bands in region of 300–400 nm and 480–600 are assigned to the n→π* (HOMO→LUMO) transition of O-related defect states (CO) and N-related defect states (CN) on the surface of CNDs, respectively. Therefore, the oxidation (o-CNDs) and reduction (r-CNDs) of CNDs surface would lead to the shifting of PL band into blue/green and red-light region, respectively.

To investigate the feasibility of the CND sample for bio-imaging application, its cytotoxicity for cells (A549 and HeLa) co-cultured with different concentrations of CNDs has been evaluated as shown in Fig. 4 a–b. Viabilities of both cell lines remains above 85% after 12 and 24 h even at high CND concentrations (400 μg/mL), confirming that the CNDs exhibit low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. Potential application of CNDs with tunable full-color luminescence as nontoxic multicolor bio-labeling probe was investigated by evaluating imaging capability of CNDs to live cell. As depicted in Fig. 4c, after being incubated with 100 μg/mL CNDs for 12 h, the live cells exhibit bright blue, green, yellow and red fluorescent emission under the excitation light of 405, 488, 514 and 543 nm, respectively. The results imply that CNDs can potentially be employed as fluorescent probes for multicolor bioimaging through endocytosis [45,46].

Moreover, no autofluorescence emerges from cells in fluorescence field (Fig. S8), which confirms that bright multicolor fluorescence is ascribed to the cell uptake of CNDs and being internalized into the cells. It is worthy to note from these images that bright fluorescence agglomerate not only in cytoplasmic area but also in nuclei and locate nearly at the whole cellular regions. These preliminary results demonstrate that the CNDs are suitable candidates for live cell imaging and tracking.

To investigate feasibility of the CND sample for in vivo bioimaging, quantification fluorescent intensity of aqueous dispersions with different concentrations of CNDs were analyzed. The quantitative relationship of fluorescence intensity versus CNDs concentration and the pseudo-color images were shown in Fig. 5 a–b. The average fluorescence signal intensity was positively correlated to CNDs concentration. It was suggested that the CNDs with concentration of 200–800 μg/mL can give a detectable and strong enough fluorescence signal for in vivo bioimaging. Fig. S6 shows a fluorescent image 5 min after subcutaneously injection into a nude mouse with 100 μL of 500 μg/mL CND dispersion. The image confirms that strong fluorescence can be observed from the CND dispersion under skin. For in vivo biodistribution studies, 200 μL of 500 μg/mL CNDs dispersion was injected into nude mice for whole body circulation through the tail vein. And the in vivo bioimaging test are successfully performed using red fluorescence emission (680 nm emission) which can efficiently penetrate skin and tissues [47]. During the whole experiments, there is no sign of acute toxicological responses in animals. In vivo fluorescence pseudo-color images at 0, 5, 15, 30, 60, 120 min post-injection were shown in Fig. 5c. At 5 min post-injection, the clear fluorescence signal with high signal-to-noise ratio was observed in whole body of the mice, suggesting that the injected CNDs underwent a blood circulation. After 15 min of circulation, the fluorescence signal gradually concentrated in the areas of liver, small intestine and large intestine. At 30 min and 60 min post-injection, a strong fluorescence signal appeared in the small intestine and bladder region, suggesting that CNDs could accumulate in small intestine or excrete through intestine and bladder system. Moreover, a very strong fluorescence signal appeared in the residual urine on the skin. After 120 min of circulation, the fluorescence signal was gradually reduced and mainly appeared in the large intestine, suggesting that most CNDs had been cleared via the circulatory system in the form of urine and some residual CNDs would be excreted through intestine in the form of feces [48]. These results indicate that the CNDs with tunable full-color luminescence possess high signal-to-noise ratio, strong tissue penetrability, and rapid excretion.

Since the introduction of carbon nanomaterials into the body may lead to subsequent organs damage involved the clearance of nanoparticle [43,49], in vivo toxicity of CNDs in various organs (including heart, liver, lung, kidney and spleen) is further evaluate by histopathological analysis (Fig. 5d). Compared with the control group, all the major organs treated with CNDs have integrated tissue structure without any inflammation and abnormal defects, which is also found in previous reports for in vivo toxicity of CNDs [1,47,48]. Histopathologically, in both control group and CNDs-treated group, cardiac myocytes are arrayed in order without any inflammatory infiltrates, hypertrophy, hemorrhage or necrosis; the liver lobules structure with central vein was clearly delineated; the lung tissue was normal with no bronchioles or alveoli collapse, alveolar epithelial denaturation, or inflammatory cell infiltration surrounding the bronchus; the renal glomerulus and various kidney tubes displayed normal shape without degeneration or bleeding; the structure of splenic corpuscle was also normal and clearly delineated, no pathological changes were found in spleen sinus, the white pulp, and red pulp of spleen. All above histopathological results suggest that CNDs are nontoxic and safe for in vivo bioimaging applications.

CNDs with tunable full-color luminescence have been successfully prepared by a solvothermal approach. Unlike previously reported CNDs, the as-prepared CNDs in this work show unusually broad absorption and fluorescence emission (nearly cover the entire visible region) with comparable fluorescence intensities. The relationship between their surface chemical state and fluorescence emission is discussed in detail. It is demonstrated that O-related defect states (CO) and N-related defect states (CN) on the surface of CNDs should be responsible for blue/green and red fluorescence emissions, respectively. These findings could provide guidance for the preparation and understanding of other CNDs with tunable full-color luminescence in future. Moreover, the CNDs are found to be biocompatible, as verified by in vitro cells assay and in vivo histological analysis and show great potentials as a multicolor bio-imaging reagent. The in vivo fluorescence imaging assay demonstrate that CNDs could excrete through intestine and bladder system. The results show that the CND florescent probe has high signal-to-noise ratio, strong tissue penetrability, rapid excretion and multicolor emission, which can potentially have versatile application in bio-imaging.

Lei Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Investigation. Xuehui Zhang: Data curation, Formal analysis, Resources. Kun Yang: Investigation, Validation. Luning Wang: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing - review & editing. Chun-Sing Lee: Project administration, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.
